title: Why Black Cats Are Unlucky 🐱 Origin and Truth
description: The myth of the unlucky black cat is older than you think. From medieval Europe to modern shelters, black cats have been blamed, hunted, and adopted last. Discover the historical origin, the religious panic, and the modern adoption data that changes everything.

segments.voice: A black cat crosses your path. Depending on where you live, that's either bad luck or good fortune. The same animal — opposite meanings. The split traces back centuries, to a real historical event that turned a common housecat into a symbol of evil for an entire continent.

Welcome to CatBrothers. The story starts in the 13th century. In 1233, Pope Gregory IX issued a papal bull called Vox in Rama. Inside that document, he linked black cats to satanic rituals and devil worship. The text described black cats as participants in heretical ceremonies.

What followed was brutal. Across Europe, black cats were hunted, killed, and burned. Entire villages organized purges. Some historians argue the population of black cats dropped so sharply in some regions that ecological balance shifted. Fewer cats meant more rats. More rats meant disease.

Here's where it gets darker. Some researchers connect the medieval cat purges to the rapid spread of the Black Death in the 14th century. Without enough cats to control rodent populations, the rats — and the fleas they carried — multiplied unchecked. A coincidence? Maybe. A contributing factor? Possibly.

The witch hunts of the 16th and 17th centuries kept the legend alive. Black cats were labeled witches' familiars — supernatural companions used to perform dark magic. Women accused of witchcraft were sometimes burned alongside their cats. The animal became guilty by association, century after century.

Not every culture turned on black cats. In England, sailors believed black cats brought safe voyages. Fishermen's wives kept black cats at home, convinced they protected their husbands at sea. The same animal — opposite belief — same century.

In Japan, black cats are still considered lucky today. The maneki-neko statue, often shown in white, also exists in black variants believed to ward off evil. In Scotland, finding a strange black cat on your porch is supposed to mean prosperity is coming. Folklore is messy.

Modern data tells another story entirely. Multiple shelter studies in the U.S., including reports from the ASPCA, have shown that black cats are adopted at lower rates than cats of other colors. They sit in shelters longer. Some end up euthanized simply for being the wrong shade.

But here's the catch. A 2013 study from the University of California, Berkeley, found that the difference in adoption rates may have been overstated. When adjusted for the higher proportion of black cats in shelters, the gap narrowed considerably. The story is real, but more complicated than headlines suggest.

Some shelters now run dedicated promotions for black cats. Black Cat Appreciation Day on August 17th was created specifically to push back against the lingering stigma. Shelters that participate report measurable bumps in black cat adoptions during that week.

Why are black cats black at all? It comes down to genetics. The dominant allele responsible for solid black fur is linked to a gene called MC1R. Surprisingly, that same gene is also associated with disease resistance in some studies. Black cats may actually have a genetic edge against certain illnesses.

A 2003 study from the National Institutes of Health hinted that mutations responsible for black coats may also confer protection against viral infections. The research is preliminary, but it raises a fascinating possibility — the very trait that made black cats hated may have made them biologically tougher.

Black cats also have golden eyes. The combination of melanin in fur and lipochromes in the iris creates that classic glowing-eye effect under low light. Photographers love them. Owners describe them as walking shadows with two miniature lanterns built into their face.

Edgar Allan Poe wrote about a black cat in 1843. The short story, simply titled The Black Cat, played heavily on the existing superstition. It became one of the most famous works in American gothic literature and helped solidify the black cat image in modern pop culture.

Halloween eventually adopted the black cat as one of its central icons. By the early 20th century, black cats were standard imagery alongside witches, ghosts, and pumpkins. The marketing reinforced what the medieval superstitions had started — a free advertising campaign for centuries of fear.

Here's an irony worth noting. Some shelters report that black cats actually have calmer, more affectionate temperaments on average. Whether that's due to genetics, the type of household that adopts them, or pure coincidence is still debated. But the reputation among rescue volunteers is consistent — black cats are surprisingly chill.

What I've noticed in my own home, sharing space with one of these midnight-colored cats, is that they're often the first to sit on your laptop and the last to leave the room. They follow you. They watch. They pick a person and they stay loyal in a way some other coats just don't match.

In Germany, the direction matters. A black cat crossing left-to-right brings bad luck. A black cat crossing right-to-left brings good luck. Same cat, same path, opposite meaning depending on the angle. It's a perfect example of how arbitrary the superstition really is.

Italy has its own twist. There, a black cat on the bed is considered a serious omen of illness. Spain takes the more romantic angle — a black cat near a wedding means the marriage will fail. Russia, on the other hand, often treats black cats as protective.

Modern psychology offers a simpler explanation for why so many cultures developed dark beliefs around black cats. The pareidolia effect — the human brain's tendency to see meaning in randomness — combines with the natural creepiness of glowing eyes in the dark to create exactly the kind of legend that spreads.

Cats themselves, of course, have no idea any of this exists. Your black cat doesn't care about omens or papal bulls. They want food, sun, and a place to nap. Their reputation is entirely a human invention, projected onto an animal that's just being a cat.

Today, awareness is shifting. Social media has fueled a wave of black cat appreciation accounts on Instagram and TikTok. Veterinary clinics report rising adoption interest. The medieval propaganda is finally losing its grip after almost 800 years of damage.

If you're considering adopting a cat, give the black ones a chance. They wait longer. They get overlooked. And in shelters, that means they often go home with people who actively seek them out — which tends to result in deeper, more deliberate bonds.

The myth was never about the cat. It was about humans, fear, and a single religious decree that echoed across centuries. Once you understand that, every black cat you meet becomes a survivor. A descendant of generations that endured purges, plagues, and propaganda.

Subscribe to CatBrothers and hit the bell. What's coming next week — how cats actually see colors, with the latest research on feline vision and how it differs from ours. See you in the next documentary.
